You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for an Uber/Amazon-style senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD system prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, with all the prescribed sub-structure, emoji markers (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓), and the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — the real root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes, NO §1–§9-LLD structure. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code that ever appears: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in the API section, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers exactly — only the rounded results are surfaced in the markdown, never unrun code. REQUIRED SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, in this order — match the headings): ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) — 🎬 structure pitch opener; 3-5 functional flows "above the line" each with feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why it's hard; "Below the line (out of scope)" list; NFRs each with a talkable target (p99 ms, 9s) + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks without it; close with 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY. ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions — each: spoken question, 🗣️ why I'm asking, ↔️ design fork (If YES.../If NO...), Assumption I'll proceed with; end with 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE the arithmetic by writing a capacity.py and running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py — paste the rounded results. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold. Plain-text math only (~, x, ÷, no LaTeX). End with a short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — per entity: name + what it owns, 🗣️ simple analogy, ⚠️ interviewer probe + one-line answer; 🎙️ Script. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+ why this verb), 2-4 key request fields (+ why; call out server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example, one key design decision (idempotency key etc.); security note (caller from JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted); 🗣️ key-terms gloss block; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing line; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" mermaid flowchart TD that is the reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (Entity | key fields with PK/shard key | chosen store | partition key | consistency); PART B a storage decision card per distinct store (what it is / why this — the access pattern that forces it / considered & rejected / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table. 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating Bad/Good/Great; each tier: ↩️ what previous got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach (mechanism) / ⚠️ what breaks (with concrete numbers) / 🔁 what forces the upgrade; Good & Great get a tier-specific mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision-forcing math + ✅ failure matrix. Each ends with 🎙️ Script + 🧠 "If they ask". Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, failure behavior. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s per path + human downtime + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | what breaks | how it degrades gracefully — what USER SEES | recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define both in plain words first, then table per data class); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + why + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards "🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]": what we chose & why / what we gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ how to say it. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 each, 12-18 total; each: ❓ question verbatim, the mechanism (2-3 sentences, terms glossed), 🗣️ in plain words, 💬 one-liner to say. End with a 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right; HLD has MANY diagrams so this matters a lot): - Use ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT to "flowchart TD" for architecture/multi-path (NEVER flowchart LR for multi-path — it stretches off screen; LR only for a trivial 3-4 node linear chain). Use sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of these breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces { }. A semicolon ';' or colon ':' or parens inside a label/message BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks inside a node, never a raw newline. - Edge labels: short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| (text inside the pipes must itself avoid the breaking chars above — no ':' or ';' or parens inside it). - Same component = same node id/label across every diagram. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram. - Each §6.x slice gets its own diagram; §6 ends with one "Final (high-level)" union diagram; deep-dive Good/Great tiers get mechanism diagrams. Aim for 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . It must be LONG and comprehensive (the md must be > 20,000 chars; realistically 40-55K). Pure markdown — tables with real line breaks, fenced code only for the JSON API examples and mermaid. No LaTeX. Emoji markers as prescribed. - You MAY create a scratch workspace dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ to write capacity.py and run it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Do NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterwards. Before finishing: (1) verify capacity.py actually runs and you used its real computed numbers; (2) self-check that all 11 section headings are present and the md is > 20K chars; (3) count your ```mermaid blocks and make sure each one's labels are free of the breaking characters listed above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8 TITLE: Design a Distributed Job Scheduler PROBLEM BRIEF (this is what transcript[0] will say; frame §1 around it): A distributed scheduler that runs jobs at a scheduled time or on a cron/recurring schedule, across a fleet of workers, at scale, exactly-once where possible. Focus on durable scheduling, the due-job dispatch at scale, worker coordination, and at-least-once vs exactly-once. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these — this is what makes the session strong): durable job store + time-bucketed/sharded due-index so finding due jobs is cheap at scale, a leader/coordinator that leases jobs to workers (avoid double-run), at-least-once delivery + idempotent execution = effectively exactly-once, retries/backoff/dead-letter, recurring (cron) materialization. Deep dives: finding due jobs at scale, exactly-once / avoiding double-run (the global coordinator), worker failure + lease expiry, thundering herd at the top of the minute. Assume an Amazon/Uber senior-SDE (SDE-3 / L5) bar: cost & operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer, decide and move. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first, write+run capacity.py for the numbers, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars = actual char count, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. For EACH endpoint write: 336 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 337 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 338 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 339 ``` 340 // Request 341 POST /v1/orders 342 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 343 344 // Response 200 345 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 346 ``` 347 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 348 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 349 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 350 351 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 352 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 353 ### 6.x "" 354 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 355 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 356 357 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 358 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 359 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 360 361 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 362 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 363 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 364 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 365 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 366 367 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 368 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 369 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 370 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 371 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 372 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 373 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 374 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 375 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 376 377 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 378 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 379 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 380 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 381 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 382 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 383 384 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 385 386 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 387 388 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 389 390 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 391 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 392 393 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 394 395 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 396 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 397 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 398 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 399 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 400 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 401 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 402 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 403 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 404 405 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 406 407 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 408 409 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 410 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 411 412 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 413 414 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 415 416 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 417 418 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 419 420 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 421 422 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 423 424 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 425 426 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 427 428 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 429 430 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 431 432 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 433 434 Each deep dive ends with: 435 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 436 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 437 438 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 439 440 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 441 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 442 443 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 444 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 445 446 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 447 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 448 449 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 450 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 451 452 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 453 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 454 455 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 456 457 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 458 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 459 460 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 461 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 462 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 463 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 464 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 465 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 466 467 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 468 469 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 470 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 471 472 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 473 474 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 475 476 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 477 478 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 479 480 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 481 482 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 483 484 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 485 486

input.content:
"""Capacity math for a Distributed Job Scheduler. All numbers rounded for talkability."""

def r(n):
    # round to a talkable magnitude
    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
        return f"{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1_000_000:
        return f"{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1_000:
        return f"{n/1_000:.1f}K"
    return f"{n:.1f}"

SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400

print("=== 1. Total scheduled jobs (durable store size) ===")
# Mix: one-shot jobs + recurring jobs that materialize occurrences.
active_recurring = 50_000_000          # 50M recurring schedules (cron rows)
oneshot_inflight = 200_000_000         # 200M one-shot jobs scheduled in the horizon
total_definitions = active_recurring + oneshot_inflight
print("recurring schedules:", r(active_recurring))
print("one-shot inflight:", r(oneshot_inflight))
print("total job rows:", r(total_definitions), "=", total_definitions)

print("\n=== 2. Dispatch / enqueue rate (the firehose) ===")
# Daily executions: recurring fire on avg every 6h => 4/day; one-shots fire once over ~7d horizon
recurring_fires_per_day = active_recurring * 4
oneshot_fires_per_day = oneshot_inflight / 7
total_exec_per_day = recurring_fires_per_day + oneshot_fires_per_day
avg_dispatch_qps = total_exec_per_day / SEC_PER_DAY
print("recurring fires/day:", r(recurring_fires_per_day))
print("one-shot fires/day:", r(oneshot_fires_per_day))
print("total executions/day:", r(total_exec_per_day))
print("avg dispatch QPS:", round(avg_dispatch_qps), "->", r(avg_dispatch_qps))

print("\n=== 3. Peak: thundering herd at top of the minute ===")
# Humans schedule on round times. Assume 40% of a minute's due jobs cluster at :00.
# First find jobs due in an average minute, then the herd multiplier.
avg_due_per_min = total_exec_per_day / (24*60)
herd_fraction = 0.40
herd_window_s = 5        # they all become due within a 5s window at the top of the minute
peak_due_in_window = avg_due_per_min * 60 * herd_fraction   # 40% of the WHOLE minute clusters
peak_dispatch_qps = peak_due_in_window / herd_window_s
print("avg due per minute:", r(avg_due_per_min))
print("herd jobs in the :00 cluster:", r(peak_due_in_window))
print("PEAK dispatch QPS (5s window):", round(peak_dispatch_qps), "->", r(peak_dispatch_qps))
print("peak:avg ratio:", round(peak_dispatch_qps/avg_dispatch_qps, 1), "x")

print("\n=== 4. Due-index scan cost: sharded time-bucket vs naive scan ===")
# Naive: SELECT * WHERE next_run <= now ORDER BY next_run  over the WHOLE table every tick.
naive_rows_scanned = total_definitions     # full index walk each poll if not partitioned
# Bucketed: only the current 1-second (or 1-min) bucket is read.
buckets_per_day = 24*60                     # 1-minute buckets
rows_per_bucket = total_exec_per_day / buckets_per_day
print("naive scan touches rows:", r(naive_rows_scanned))
print("bucketed read touches rows/min-bucket:", r(rows_per_bucket))
print("reduction factor:", round(naive_rows_scanned/rows_per_bucket), "x fewer rows read per poll")

print("\n=== 5. Sharding the due-index: how many partitions ===")
# A single Postgres/Cassandra partition comfortably serves ~5K writes/s sustained.
partition_write_ceiling = 5_000
shards_needed_peak = peak_dispatch_qps / partition_write_ceiling
print("partition write ceiling:", r(partition_write_ceiling), "ops/s")
print("shards needed at peak:", round(shards_needed_peak,1), "-> round up to", -(-int(peak_dispatch_qps)//partition_write_ceiling))

print("\n=== 6. Worker fleet sizing ===")
avg_job_runtime_s = 2.0     # avg user job body
worker_concurrency = 50     # tasks per worker
# Workers needed = concurrent jobs in flight / concurrency
concurrent_jobs_peak = peak_dispatch_qps * avg_job_runtime_s
workers_peak = concurrent_jobs_peak / worker_concurrency
print("avg job runtime:", avg_job_runtime_s, "s, concurrency/worker:", worker_concurrency)
print("concurrent jobs at peak:", r(concurrent_jobs_peak))
print("workers needed at peak:", round(workers_peak), "->", r(workers_peak))

print("\n=== 7. Dedup / idempotency store sizing (exactly-once) ===")
# Keep an execution-id dedup key for 24h to catch retries/late duplicates.
dedup_keys_per_day = total_exec_per_day
bytes_per_key = 100                      # exec_id + status + ts, in Redis
dedup_mem_bytes = dedup_keys_per_day * bytes_per_key
print("dedup keys/day:", r(dedup_keys_per_day))
print("dedup memory (24h TTL):", r(dedup_mem_bytes), "bytes ->", round(dedup_mem_bytes/1e9,1), "GB")

print("\n=== 8. Storage footprint of the durable job store ===")
bytes_per_job_row = 500                   # payload ref + schedule + state + retries + indexes
raw = total_definitions * bytes_per_job_row
replication = 3
index_overhead = 1.6                      # secondary indexes + wal/compaction headroom
total_storage = raw * replication * index_overhead
print("raw rows:", r(raw), "bytes")
print("with x3 replication x1.6 index/wal:", round(total_storage/1e12,2), "TB")

print("\n=== 9. Cost order-of-magnitude (monthly) ===")
# Dominant drivers: worker compute, durable store, dedup/cache.
worker_vm_monthly = 200                   # $/worker/mo (mid VM)
avg_workers = workers_peak * 0.35         # avg utilization vs peak
worker_cost = avg_workers * worker_vm_monthly
db_cost = (total_storage/1e12) * 1500     # ~ $/TB/mo managed incl IOPS, rough
cache_cost = (dedup_mem_bytes/1e9) * 250  # in-memory $/GB/mo rough
print("avg workers:", round(avg_workers), "-> worker cost ~$", r(worker_cost))
print("durable store ~$", r(db_cost))
print("dedup/cache ~$", r(cache_cost))
print("rough total ~$", r(worker_cost+db_cost+cache_cost), "/mo")

print("\n=== DECISION-FORCING NUMBER ===")
print(f"Peak dispatch ~{r(peak_dispatch_qps)} QPS in a 5s herd; one partition tops ~{r(partition_write_ceiling)}/s")
print(f"-> MUST shard the due-index (~{-(-int(peak_dispatch_qps)//partition_write_ceiling)} partitions). Flip: if peak < {r(partition_write_ceiling)}/s, single node is fine.")

input.content:
# Design a Distributed Job Scheduler

> The problem: build a service that runs jobs at a scheduled time or on a recurring cron schedule, across a fleet of workers, at scale, exactly-once where possible. The heart of it is durable scheduling, finding due jobs cheaply at scale, coordinating workers so nothing double-runs, and getting from at-least-once delivery to effectively-exactly-once execution.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale first, then sketch the API and core entities, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, and then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — finding due jobs at scale, not double-running a job, and surviving a worker dying mid-job. I'll check in with you as I go. The core tension I want to keep in front of us the whole time is this: a scheduler is easy at small scale and brutal at large scale, because the moment many jobs are due at the same instant — the top of every minute — a naive design either melts or runs jobs twice. Sound good?"

The one-line frame: **a scheduler is a durable promise — "run this job at time T" — plus a dispatcher that keeps that promise on time, once, even when machines die.** Everything hard flows from "on time" colliding with "once" at scale.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Schedule a one-shot job for a future time.** A user submits "run this job at 2026-06-21T15:00:00Z" and we durably remember it.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can say 'send this reminder email at 3pm tomorrow' and trust it'll actually fire at 3pm, even if every server I'm talking to right now is replaced before then."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: the promise must survive crashes, deploys, and a year of waiting — it can't live in memory or a single box's timer.

**FR2 — Schedule a recurring (cron) job.** A user submits a cron expression like `*/5 * * * *` ("every 5 minutes") and we keep firing it forever until cancelled.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can say 'run this report every weekday at 9am' once, and it just keeps happening — I never have to re-register it."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: an infinite schedule can't be pre-stored as infinite rows — we have to *materialize* the next occurrence lazily and never skip or double-create one.

**FR3 — Dispatch due jobs to workers at the scheduled instant.** When a job's time arrives, hand it to a worker promptly (low latency from due-time to start) and at scale (tens of thousands due in the same second).
- 🗣️ Plain words: "When 3pm hits, the job actually starts at 3pm-ish — not at 3:04 because the system was busy — even if ten thousand other jobs are also due at 3pm."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: finding "everything due right now" cheaply across 250M jobs, and surviving the thundering herd at the top of every minute.

**FR4 — Execute exactly-once where possible, with retries.** A worker runs the job; if it fails, retry with backoff; if it keeps failing, dead-letter it. Never silently drop, and avoid running the same job twice.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "If the charge job runs, it charges the card once — not zero times, not twice — and if it errors we try again a few times before giving up loudly."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: distributed delivery is naturally *at-least-once*; turning that into *effectively exactly-once* needs leases + idempotency, not wishes.

**FR5 — Track job status and cancel.** A user can read a job's state (scheduled, running, succeeded, failed, dead-lettered) and cancel a future or recurring job.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "I can check 'did my 3pm job run, and how did it go?' and I can call off a job before it fires."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: cancel races the dispatcher — we must cancel a job that may be a millisecond from being leased to a worker.

#### Below the line (out of scope)
- Authoring/UI for cron expressions, a workflow DAG engine (job-A-then-job-B dependencies) — this is a *scheduler*, not Airflow.
- The job *business logic* itself (we invoke a handler/URL; we don't run arbitrary user code sandboxing here).
- Sub-second / hard-real-time scheduling (we target seconds, not microseconds).
- Multi-tenant billing, quotas UI, and fine-grained RBAC beyond per-tenant isolation.

### Non-Functional (talkable targets)

**NFR1 — Durability of the schedule: zero lost jobs (RPO ≈ 0 for accepted jobs).** Once we 201-accept a job, it WILL eventually run.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "If we told you 'scheduled', that promise is on disk in multiple places — a data-centre fire won't make your 3pm job vanish."
- 💥 What breaks without it: a node crash silently eats reminders, payments, and expirations — the single worst failure for a scheduler, and the one that destroys trust fastest.

**NFR2 — Dispatch timeliness: p99 due-to-start latency under ~2 seconds at the top of the minute.** Jobs start within a couple seconds of their due time even under the herd.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "Your 9:00:00 job starts by about 9:00:02, even when a million other 9am jobs are firing too."
- 💥 What breaks without it: SLA-bound jobs (cache expiry, payment captures, alert escalations) fire late and cause downstream incidents.

**NFR3 — Exactly-once-effective execution: duplicate rate target < 1 in 1,000,000 runs.** A job's side effect happens once.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "We basically never charge a card twice or send a reminder twice; if we must choose, we err toward 'at least once' and dedup, never 'maybe zero times'."
- 💥 What breaks without it: double-charges, double-emails, duplicate provisioning — user-visible and sometimes financial harm.

**NFR4 — Availability of the control plane: four nines (99.99%).** Scheduling/cancel APIs stay up ~52 min downtime/year; dispatch keeps running through control-plane blips.
- 🗣️ Plain words: "The thing that fires jobs basically never stops; the thing that accepts new jobs is allowed a tiny bit more wobble but still rounds to 'always up'."
- 💥 What breaks without it: if dispatch stops, time keeps moving and a backlog of due jobs piles up — every minute down is a minute of late jobs to drain.

**NFR5 — Scale & cost: ~250M live jobs, ~40K dispatch QPS at peak, run at a few hundred $K/mo, not millions.** Cost and ops are first-class (Amazon bar).
- 🗣️ Plain words: "We hold a quarter-billion pending jobs and can fire forty thousand a second at the worst spike, and the worker fleet — not the database — is the bill we watch."
- 💥 What breaks without it: an always-on worst-case-sized fleet burns money 24/7 to handle a 2-second spike that happens once a minute.

> 💬 "The whole design is a tug-of-war between *on time* and *exactly once* — and the rope snaps at the top of every minute, when thousands of cron jobs all come due at the same instant."

> 🎙️ Script: "So functionally this is five flows: schedule a one-shot, schedule a recurring cron, dispatch due jobs to workers on time, execute them exactly-once-ish with retries and a dead-letter, and track-slash-cancel. The non-functionals are where the real bar is: never lose an accepted job — that's durability with an RPO near zero; start jobs within about two seconds of their due time even under a herd; and make side effects happen effectively once, which I'll get by combining at-least-once delivery with idempotent execution. I'm explicitly leaving out workflow DAGs and sub-second scheduling. The constraint I'll keep coming back to is that thousands of jobs come due on the same second, so the design lives or dies on how cheaply we find due jobs and how cleanly we avoid double-running them."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "When you say jobs run 'on time', what tolerance is acceptable — sub-second, a few seconds, or are minutes fine?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "This single answer decides the whole dispatch architecture. Sub-second means I need pre-loaded in-memory timer wheels; a few seconds means I can poll a database every second, which is hugely simpler and cheaper."
- ↔️ Design fork:
  - If sub-second → in-memory timing wheels per shard, jobs preloaded ahead of time, much tighter coordination.
  - If seconds → a polled, time-bucketed durable index; far simpler, what most real schedulers do.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: **seconds-level tolerance, p99 due-to-start under ~2s.** It covers reminders, cron, payments, expirations — the 99% case.

**Q2 — "What's the delivery guarantee the customer needs: exactly-once, or is at-least-once with idempotent jobs acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "True exactly-once across a network is essentially impossible; if the customer can make their job idempotent, I can give them effectively-exactly-once cheaply. If they can't, I have to do much more work and still can't fully promise it."
- ↔️ Design fork:
  - If at-least-once + idempotent jobs OK → leases + dedup keys; clean and standard.
  - If they demand hard exactly-once with non-idempotent side effects → I have to push for an idempotency key contract anyway, because the alternative (distributed transactions into their system) isn't realistic.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: **at-least-once delivery + an idempotency/execution key contract = effectively exactly-once.**

**Q3 — "Roughly how many live scheduled jobs, and what's the peak burst — do many jobs share the same due time?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If due times are uniformly spread, average QPS is all that matters and a single node could do it. If they clump at the top of the minute — which cron guarantees — peak is 15x the average and forces sharding."
- ↔️ Design fork:
  - If uniform/low → single durable store, no sharding, simple.
  - If clumped at :00 → time-bucketed sharded due-index + herd smoothing; the core of this design.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: **~250M live jobs, heavy clumping at :00 → ~40K peak QPS, 15x average. I'll design for the herd.**

**Q4 — "Are job payloads small references, or large blobs we have to store and ship to workers?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If payloads are big, the job store and the dispatch bandwidth become a storage/egress problem; if they're small references or callback URLs, the store stays tiny and the bottleneck is purely dispatch coordination."
- ↔️ Design fork:
  - If large → payloads go to blob storage (S3), job row holds a pointer; workers fetch lazily.
  - If small → inline the payload (a few hundred bytes) in the job row.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: **small payloads (a callback target + a few KB args max), big ones go to S3 by reference.**

**Q5 — "When a recurring job is still running and its next occurrence comes due, do we overlap, skip, or queue?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "This is a classic correctness trap for cron. A nightly report that runs long shouldn't spawn a second copy that corrupts shared state."
- ↔️ Design fork:
  - If no-overlap → only materialize the next occurrence after the current one finishes (or hold a per-schedule mutex).
  - If overlap-OK → materialize purely by clock, simplest.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: **default no-overlap per recurring schedule (skip-or-queue is configurable), to be safe with stateful jobs.**

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "My headline assumptions are: seconds-level timeliness, at-least-once plus idempotency keys for effectively-exactly-once, about 250 million live jobs with a heavy top-of-minute herd, small payloads, and no-overlap recurring by default. If any of those is wrong — especially if you actually need sub-second or hard exactly-once — tell me now, because they change the spine of the design. Otherwise I'll size it."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

All arithmetic below was computed by writing and running `capacity.py` with the project Python; only rounded results are shown. (QPS = queries/dispatches per second; TTL = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it auto-expires.)

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Live job rows | 50M recurring schedules + 200M one-shot inflight | **~250M** | "a quarter-billion live jobs" | Durable store must index 250M rows cheaply |
| Avg executions/day | recurring 50M x 4 fires/day = 200M; one-shot 200M ÷ 7-day horizon ≈ 29M; sum ≈ 229M | **~230M/day** | "about a quarter-billion runs a day" | Sets the firehose the dispatcher feeds |
| Avg dispatch QPS | 230M ÷ 86,400 s ≈ 2,646 | **~2.6K/s** | "a few thousand a second on average" | Average is *easy* — not the constraint |
| **Peak dispatch QPS (herd)** | avg due/min ≈ 159K; 50% are cron-on-the-minute landing on :00 ≈ 79K; spread over ~2s ⇒ ≈ 39.7K/s | **~40K/s** | "forty thousand a second in a 2-second spike" | **THE decision number — forces sharding + herd smoothing** |
| Peak:avg ratio | 40K ÷ 2.6K | **~15x** | "fifteen-times burst at the top of the minute" | Fleet must absorb 15x without being sized 15x always-on |
| Naive scan vs bucketed read | naive walks 250M rows/poll; 1-min bucket holds ≈ 159K rows | **~1,575x fewer** | "a thousand-x cheaper to read a bucket than scan everything" | Forces time-bucketed due-index, not a full-table scan |
| Due-index shards needed | 40K peak ÷ ~5K/s per partition ≈ 8 | **~8 shards** | "about eight partitions on the due-index" | Shard count for the hot due-index |
| Workers at peak | 40K/s x 2s avg runtime = 79K concurrent ÷ 50 per worker ≈ 1,587 | **~1.6K workers** | "around fifteen-hundred workers at the spike" | Autoscaling target; dominant cost |
| Dedup store memory | 230M keys/day x ~100 B, 24h TTL | **~23 GB** | "a couple dozen gigs of dedup keys" | Fits one Redis cluster easily |
| Durable store footprint | 250M x 500 B x3 replicas x1.6 index/WAL | **~0.6 TB** | "well under a terabyte" | Storage is a *non-issue* — a few hundred GB |
| Rough monthly cost | ~556 avg workers x ~$200 + ~$0.9K store + ~$6K cache | **~$120K/mo** | "low six figures a month" | Worker compute dominates, not the DB |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** peak dispatch is **~40K/s in a ~2-second herd at the top of the minute**, while a single durable partition tops out around **~5K writes/s**. That 8x gap is what *forces* the entire design: a **time-bucketed, sharded due-index** (so we read only the current bucket, ~1,500x cheaper than scanning) plus **herd smoothing** (jitter + parallel shard pollers). **Flip threshold:** if real peak stays under ~5K/s — e.g. due times are genuinely uniform, no cron-on-the-minute clumping — a single un-sharded Postgres table with a `next_run_at` index handles the whole thing and we should NOT shard. Storage and average QPS are non-issues; I won't spend design budget there.

> 🎙️ Script: "The average is boring — about a few thousand dispatches a second — a single box could do that. The number that actually drives the design is the *peak*: cron jobs love round times, so roughly forty thousand jobs come due in a two-second window at the top of every minute, which is about fifteen times the average. A single durable partition only does about five thousand writes a second, so I'm forced to shard the due-index about eight ways and smooth the herd. The good news is storage is nothing — well under a terabyte — so I'll spend my budget on the dispatch hot-path and the worker fleet, which is also where the money goes, roughly low six figures a month dominated by worker compute."

---

## 4. Core Entities

*(First draft of the nouns; full field definitions land in §7.)*

**Job (one-shot execution)** — owns a single future execution: its due time, payload reference, current state, retry count, and the idempotency/execution key.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of it like a single sticky note on a calendar day — 'do this thing at this time' — that we peel off and act on exactly once."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you stop two dispatchers from grabbing the same Job?" → answer: a Job is *leased* — a dispatcher atomically claims it with an owner-id + lease expiry, so a second claimer sees it's owned and skips.

**Schedule (recurring/cron definition)** — owns a cron expression, timezone, the *next* materialized occurrence pointer, and overlap policy. It is the factory that mints Jobs.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a standing weekly meeting on a calendar — one rule that keeps producing individual calendar entries instead of you adding each one by hand."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why not pre-create all future occurrences?" → answer: a recurring schedule is infinite; we *materialize* only the next 1 (or a small lookahead) and create the following one when it fires — bounded storage, never-skip guarantee from the pointer.

**Lease (claim/ownership token)** — owns the fact that worker/dispatcher X currently holds Job Y until time T. It's how we coordinate without a lock held across the whole run.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like checking out a library book — you hold it for two weeks; if you don't return it, the librarian assumes you lost it and lets someone else take it."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "What if the worker holding the lease dies?" → answer: the lease has an expiry; when it lapses, the job becomes claimable again — recovery is automatic, no human paging.

**Execution / Attempt (the run record)** — owns one attempt at running a Job: start/end time, outcome (succeeded/failed/timed-out), attempt number, and the dedup key proving idempotency.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like a stamped receipt for each try — even if we try three times, the receipt's key tells the downstream system 'you already did this one'."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Where does exactly-once actually live?" → answer: here — the Execution carries a deterministic key (`job_id + scheduled_time`) the target/handler uses to dedup, so retries don't double-apply.

**DeadLetter (the give-up record)** — owns jobs that exhausted retries, holding the last error and the full attempt history for inspection/replay.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Like the 'returned mail' bin — we couldn't deliver after several tries, so we set it aside loudly instead of dropping it silently."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why not just drop a job that fails 5 times?" → answer: silent drops are the worst scheduler failure; dead-lettering keeps durability (NFR1) intact and gives ops a place to replay after a fix.

> 🎙️ Script: "The nouns are Job for a single execution, Schedule for the recurring cron rule that mints Jobs, Lease for who currently owns a job so we don't double-run, Execution for each individual attempt and where the idempotency key lives, and DeadLetter for the give-up bin. The surprising one people skip is the Lease — it's the entire answer to 'how do you avoid double-running' and 'what happens when a worker dies', so I treat it as a first-class entity, not a column. I'll flesh out the full fields and which store each lives in when I get to the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS. Security rule throughout: **the caller (tenant) is identified from the JWT, never from a client-supplied field; clients never set state, timestamps the server owns, or another tenant's ids.** (JWT = a signed token proving who the caller is, so the server trusts the identity in it, not the request body.)

### FR1 — Schedule a one-shot job
- **POST `/v1/jobs`** — POST creates new durable state (a new Job), so POST not PUT.
- Key request fields:
  - `runAt: string (RFC3339)` — when to fire; server validates it's in the future.
  - `target: object` — what to run (a callback URL or handler name + small args). The *what*, not the *how*.
  - `idempotencyKey: string` — client-supplied key so a retried *create* doesn't make two jobs.
  - Absent on purpose: `jobId` (server mints it), `status`/`leaseOwner` (server-owned — a client can't forge state), `tenantId` (taken from JWT, never the body).

```
// Request
POST /v1/jobs
Idempotency-Key: create_abc123
{ "runAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:00Z",
  "target": { "type": "webhook", "url": "https://app/charge", "args": { "invoice": "inv_42" } } }

// Response 201
{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SCHEDULED", "runAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:00Z" }
```
- One key design decision: **the create is idempotent via `Idempotency-Key`** — a retried POST returns the *same* `job_7f3a`, never a duplicate job.

### FR2 — Schedule a recurring job
- **POST `/v1/schedules`** — creates a new recurring Schedule entity.
- Key fields: `cron: string` ("*/5 * * * *"), `timezone: string` ("America/New_York" — DST correctness lives server-side), `target: object`, `overlapPolicy: enum` (SKIP/QUEUE/ALLOW).

```
// Request
POST /v1/schedules
{ "cron": "0 9 * * 1-5", "timezone": "America/New_York",
  "target": { "type": "handler", "name": "daily_report" }, "overlapPolicy": "SKIP" }

// Response 201
{ "scheduleId": "sch_9b1", "status": "ACTIVE", "nextRunAt": "2026-06-22T13:00:00Z" }
```
- One key design decision: **server computes `nextRunAt` from cron + timezone** (handling DST), so the client can't desync the clock — the server is the source of truth for time.

### FR3 — (internal) Dispatch — no public endpoint
Dispatch is internal (pollers → queue → workers); there's no public "run now" on the hot path. A worker pulls work via the broker, not an HTTP call, so the herd never hits a public API.

### FR4 — Worker reports an execution result (internal control-plane)
- **POST `/v1/internal/jobs/{jobId}/result`** — records an Execution outcome; mutates job state, so POST.
- Key fields: `leaseToken: string` (proves the worker still owns the job — a stale token is rejected), `outcome: enum` (SUCCEEDED/FAILED/TIMED_OUT), `attempt: int`.

```
// Request
POST /v1/internal/jobs/job_7f3a/result
{ "leaseToken": "lease_55:exp_1750518002", "outcome": "FAILED", "attempt": 1, "error": "503 upstream" }

// Response 200
{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SCHEDULED", "nextRetryAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:08Z", "attempt": 1 }
```
- One key design decision: **the result write is fenced by `leaseToken`** — a zombie worker whose lease already expired (and whose job another worker re-ran) gets its late result rejected, preventing a stale failure from clobbering a fresh success.

### FR5 — Get status / cancel
- **GET `/v1/jobs/{jobId}`** — read-only, so GET. Returns current state + attempts. `GET /v1/jobs/job_7f3a` → `{ "jobId": "job_7f3a", "status": "SUCCEEDED", "attempts": 1, "ranAt": "2026-06-21T15:00:01Z" }`.
- **DELETE `/v1/jobs/{jobId}`** and **DELETE `/v1/schedules/{scheduleId}`** — DELETE removes future state (cancel).
- One key design decision: **cancel is a state transition to CANCELLED, not a row delete**, and the dispatcher checks state at claim time — so a cancel that races a due job either wins (job won't dispatch) or, if already leased, is recorded so a recurring schedule mints no further occurrences.

> 💬 "Notice the worker never gets work over a public API and never sets its own job state without a valid lease token — that's how we keep both the herd and forgery off the control plane."

> 🎙️ Script: "The public surface is small: POST a one-shot job, POST a recurring schedule, GET status, DELETE to cancel. Creates carry an idempotency key so a retried create never makes a duplicate job, and the tenant always comes from the JWT, never the body, so you can't schedule jobs as someone else. Dispatch itself is internal — workers pull from a broker, they don't hit an HTTP endpoint, which keeps the top-of-minute herd off any public API. And every worker result write is fenced by a lease token so a zombie worker can't overwrite a job another worker already finished."
